documents.source: “Why do Japan’s businessmen insist on war against Russia?,” editorial published in the Tokyo Keizai zasshi (Tokyo Economist), Japan’s leading business journal, 1903 “Today, we join the countless private Japanese entrepreneurs and industrialists who are advocating for a war against Russia over the Manchurian problem
documents.content:
In recent days, we have heard reports that Russia has
introduced new regulations increasing the tax it levies on non-Russian ships docking at the harbor in Port
Arthur,1so that Japanese ships would have to pay more than 12 times the amount of port tax that Russian
ships do. If Russia occupies other parts of Manchuria [a region of East Asia to the north of Korea and to the
northeast of China] and extends this method of levying taxes there, Japanese businesses may have to endure
terrible hardships, possibly cutting us out of the Manchurian trade altogether.
In short, as long as Russia retains a foothold in Manchuria, we must recognize on the basis of her past record
that she will do all in her power to hold Japan’s trade down. It is better to take the initiative and force a
solution than to wait until circumstances have further forced our hand.”
1 A major commercial hub and point of entry of foreign goods into Manchuria, Port Arthur had been leased by the
Qing Empire to Russia since 1898.

documents.source: Fumimaro Konoe, diplomat and advisor to the prime minister of Japan, “Reject the Anglo-American-Centered Peace,” essay published in the political magazine Japan and the Japanese, Tokyo, 1918
documents.content:
“As the history of England and France shows, they long ago occupied the less civilized regions of the world,
made them into colonies, and had no problem monopolizing them for their own profit. Therefore, not only
Germany but all late-developing countries were in the position of having no land to acquire.
England has lost no time in trumpeting its self-sufficiency, and now it, together with America, is advocating
that other countries be denied access to colonies. Such are the contradictions between what England and
America say and what they do. If the principle that no country should establish new colonies is carried out at
the coming peace conference,2 it would be a great economic blow to Japan. Germany’s need to overthrow
this order was quite justified, though the means it chose were unfortunate because they were based on
militarism. Nevertheless, as a Japanese I cannot help feeling deep sympathy for what Germany had to do.
Japan is limited in territory, poor in natural resources, and has a meager domestic market for manufactured
products. If England closed off its colonies, how would we be able to assure our nation’s secure survival? In
such a case, the need to ensure its survival would compel Japan to attempt to overthrow the status quo as
Germany did before the war.”
2 Konoe was a member of Japan’s delegation to the 1919–1920 Paris Peace Conference.

documents.source: Manifesto Against the Japanese Invasion of Manchuria, published by The Allied Korean Organizations in New York, a community organization of Korean Americans, 1931
documents.content:
“Japan gained her foothold in the continent when she annexed Korea in 1910 regardless of her repeated
solemn vows to recognize the sovereignty and independence of the Korean Kingdom. Japan violated so
many treaties and agreements that it is absurd to believe that there is any sincerity in Japan’s motive to abide
with or to live up to any kind of international treaties or agreements in which she has been or will be one of
the participants. After the annexation of Korea under the pretext of Asia for Asians, Japan has been steadily
looking for a chance to make Manchuria a second victim of her shameless imperialistic conquest. One major
railroad in Manchuria was already built with Japanese capital, and another one is almost halfway completed.
It is only a matter of time before the most fertile region of Manchuria would be entirely under the Japanese
domination.”

documents.source: Kanji Ishiwara, Japanese army officer stationed in Manchuria, “Personal Opinion on the Manchuria-Mongolia Problem,” essay written in 1931, shortly before he masterminded the diplomatic crisis that led to the Japanese occupation of Manchuria
documents.content:
“For a nation-state to play an active role in the world, its most essential requirement is a favorable national
defense position. As American economic power advances, the United States will become the champion of the
Western peoples. Our country must resist the encroachments of Russia to the north as it simultaneously
confronts British and American power to the south. Northern Manchuria is of strategic value to Japan. If our
country brings northern Manchuria under its influence, Russia will find it extremely difficult to advance to
the east. It will not be difficult to block Russia simply by building up our strength in Manchuria and
Mongolia. If our country is relieved of its burden to the north, it can then make bold plans for China and the
South Sea region.
The Manchuria-Mongolia region is of enormous strategic importance with respect to the destiny and
development of our country. If the Manchuria-Mongolia region is brought under our influence, then our
control over Korea will be stabilized. If our country shows firm determination in resolving the
Manchuria-Mongolia problem through force, it can assume a position of leadership toward China; it can
promote China’s unity and stability; and it can guarantee peace in the East.”

documents.source: Eliot Janeway, United States journalist, article published in the New York Times, 1937
documents.content:
“Japan’s new offensive in the Beijing region3 is her answer to the question upon which the future of Asia
apparently hinges: Who is to use the abundant raw materials of China? Both China and Japan greatly desire
these materials. If Japan prevails in this, it will mean far more than the shrinking of China’s boundaries. The
industrialization of China will be fatally hampered by the loss of this principal source of raw materials. Japan
requires the products of this area for its own uses, but even if she did not, she would have had to strike here
in order to prevent China’s industrialization and the unification which she fears would result from it.
The Shaanxi province has the very commodities that Japan needs most desperately. If Japan’s steel industry
is to become self-sufficient, it will need the 300,000,000 tons of ore which lie in Shaanxi. Likewise, Japan is
facing coal shortages. The coal her iron and steel industries use must be imported, and again Shaanxi is the
nearest source.”
3 A reference to the fighting during the Second Sino-Japanese War (1937–1945), in which Japan invaded China and
fought against a coalition of Nationalist and Communist Chinese forces

documents.source: Toichi Nawa, Japanese economist and professor at the Osaka University of Commerce, The Japanese Cotton Spinning Industry and the Question of the Supply of Raw Cotton, book published in Osaka, 1937
documents.content:
“Would it be possible, as many have suggested, for Japan to put North China under our control so that we
have full access to her resources, including her rich cotton fields? The answer, in my view, is “no.” China is a
backward society, based on a traditional pattern of landholding and lacking modern industries. It would
require extraordinarily large expenditures to develop its economy, block interventions from third countries,
and develop policies to mitigate Chinese resistance. It is highly doubtful that Japan has the necessary capital
and resources to cope with such a monumental task.
The foreign trade statistics of the last few years show that the more we expand the productive capacity of our
industries in preparation to extend Japanese rule to mainland Asia, the greater our dependence on the world
market and the imports of raw materials becomes. The lure of empire building in Asia is therefore a grave
danger for our country’s economic advance.”

documents.source: Japanese language class being taught by a Japanese instructor at a Singaporean school, circa 1943. Photograph published in Asahi Shimbun, one of Japan’s major daily newspapers. Asahi Shimbun / Getty Images
documents.content:
The British colony of Singapore fell to the Japanese armies in 1942.
END OF DOCUMENTS FOR QUESTION 1

parts.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which economic motives were the leading cause of Japanese imperialism in the period
circa 1900–1945.
parts.scoring_notes: Full rubric: Thesis (1), Contextualization (1), Evidence from Documents (2), Evidence Beyond Documents (1), Sourcing (1), Complexity (1). Total: 7 points.

scoring_points.description: Thesis: Historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for a historically defensible thesis/claim about the extent to which economic motives were the LEADING cause of Japanese imperialism circa 1900–1945. Must provide some indication of the reason or establish analytic categories. Not earned for restating the prompt, providing a defensible claim without line of reasoning, or making a historically indefensible claim.

sample_responses.response: Taking advantage of its newly industrialized economy, Japan established a colonial empire in order to compete with Western imperial powers by securing the raw materials and markets that its domestic economy lacked.
sample_responses.commentary: Establishes a line of reasoning: industrialization → need for resources/markets → imperial expansion. Addresses economic motive as primary cause. CB-aligned thesis example.

sample_responses.response: The motives behind Japanese imperialism in 1900–1945 included the desire to secure natural resources for its economy, the determination to demonstrate it could rival European states as an imperial power, and a sense of Japanese cultural and racial superiority over other East and Southeast Asian peoples.
sample_responses.commentary: Establishes analytic categories (economic resources, status competition, racial ideology) that allow evaluation of economic motives' relative importance. A stronger thesis that directly addresses extent.

sample_responses.response: Economic motives were the leading cause of Japanese imperialism in the period circa 1900–1945.
sample_responses.commentary: Simply restates the prompt. Does not establish a line of reasoning or provide any indication of why economic motives were (or were not) leading.

required_elements: Historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning about economic motives and Japanese imperialism.
alternatives.correct_example: Taking advantage of its newly industrialized economy, Japan established a colonial empire in order to compete with Western imperial powers by securing raw materials and markets.

wrong_examples: A simple restatement of the prompt without taking a position.
A vague claim that does not establish a line of reasoning.

scoring_points.description: Contextualization: Describe a broader historical context relevant to the prompt.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for accurately describing a broader historical context relevant to economic motives and Japanese imperialism circa 1900–1945. Must be more than a phrase. Relevant contexts include: Japanese isolationist policies during Tokugawa period; Japanese contacts with the West during late Tokugawa and early Meiji; Meiji Restoration industrialization and modernization; European imperial expansion in China (Opium Wars, spheres of influence, unequal treaties); growth of Japanese nationalism; economic impacts of the Great Depression in Asia; rising anti-Western sentiment after WWI.

sample_responses.response: The Meiji Restoration in Japan (1868) transformed Japan from a feudal society into an industrialized nation-state by abolishing the shogunate, eliminating samurai privileges, and building Western-style industries and military forces. This industrialization gave Japan both the capability to project military power and the economic need for raw materials and markets that its limited domestic territory could not provide — creating the material basis for imperial ambitions.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the Meiji Restoration as the specific historical context that created both the means and motivations for Japanese imperialism. More than a phrase — provides elaborated description. CB-aligned.

sample_responses.response: The Meiji period turned Japan into a major industrial power.
sample_responses.commentary: Minimally acceptable contextualization per the CB guideline. Brief but identifies the relevant context.

sample_responses.response: Japan's monarchy is the oldest in the world.
sample_responses.commentary: Not relevant to the topic of economic motives for Japanese imperialism.

required_elements: Broader historical context relevant to Japanese industrialization, competition with Western powers, or motivations for imperial expansion.
alternatives.correct_example: The Meiji Restoration transformed Japan's economy and created both the capability and the need for imperial expansion to secure resources and markets.

wrong_examples: A passing reference to a historical event without elaboration.
Context that is not relevant to Japanese imperialism or economic motives.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Docs: Use content of at least three documents to address the topic of the prompt.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for accurately describing — rather than simply quoting — the content of at least three documents to address the topic of economic motives and Japanese imperialism. Not earned for: fewer than three documents, misinterpretation, simple quotation without description, or addressing documents collectively.

sample_responses.response: Document 1 describes how Japanese businessmen supported war with Russia because Russian taxes on Japanese ships at Port Arthur threatened to cut Japan out of Manchurian trade. Document 3 argues that Japan violated Korean sovereignty and is pursuing imperialist conquest in Manchuria. Document 6 argues that Japan should not try to control North China because the country is too 'backward' and would require enormous investments to develop.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes the content of three documents (1, 3, 6) all relevant to economic motives for Japanese imperialism. Each is described, not simply quoted.

sample_responses.response: The documents discuss Japan's imperialism in Asia between 1900 and 1945.
sample_responses.commentary: Addresses documents collectively rather than describing each individually. Does not describe the content of any specific document.

required_elements: Accurate description of at least three documents' content relevant to Japanese imperialism and economic motives.
alternatives.correct_example: Describes three or more documents' content individually, addressing the topic of economic motives for Japanese imperialism.

wrong_examples: Simply quoting documents without describing them.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Docs+: Support an argument using at least four documents.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for supporting an argument about the extent to which economic motives were the leading cause of Japanese imperialism by accurately using content from at least four documents. Documents can support sub-arguments or counterarguments.

sample_responses.response: Document 4 shows Ishiwara believes that seizing Manchuria and Mongolia would allow Japan to assume Asian leadership, contain Russia, and deal with Anglo-American challenges — demonstrating that leaders considered both economic and strategic/military factors in advocating for expansion. Document 5 shows Janeway implies Japan's invasion of China was motivated by controlling coal and iron resources, supporting an argument about economic resource competition. Document 1 shows Japanese businessmen feared Russian taxes would cut them out of Manchurian trade, supporting the economic motivation argument. Document 2 shows Konoe argued Japan needed colonies for economic self-sufficiency like Britain and France had achieved.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses four documents (1, 2, 4, 5) to support arguments about economic motives, with each document connected to a specific argument about the extent or nature of Japan's economic motivations. CB-aligned.

required_elements: Uses at least four documents to support arguments about economic motives and Japanese imperialism.
alternatives.correct_example: Connects four documents to specific arguments about economic motives' role in Japanese imperialism.

wrong_examples: Merely describing four documents without connecting them to an argument.
Using only three documents in support of an argument.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Beyond: Use at least one additional piece of specific historical evidence beyond the documents.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for using at least one specific piece of historical evidence beyond the documents relevant to economic motives and Japanese imperialism circa 1900–1945. Must be specific, different from documents, and different from evidence used for contextualization. Acceptable evidence: Meiji Era reforms/industrialization not in documents; development of textile/chemical industries; Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere; American oil embargo against Japan; defeat of European colonial powers by Germany and effects on Asian colonies; Pearl Harbor, Philippines invasion; Japanese atrocities (forced Korean labor, comfort women, Nanjing).

sample_responses.response: The defeat of France and the Netherlands by Germany in 1940 gave Japan an opportunity to seize their Asian colonies — Vietnam and Indonesia — which were rich in oil, rubber, and rice. This strategic seizure of resource-rich European colonies shows how economic resource acquisition drove Japanese imperial expansion beyond Manchuria.
sample_responses.commentary: Specific evidence not in any of the seven documents: the 1940 German conquest and its effect on European colonies in Southeast Asia. Directly relevant to economic motives for Japanese expansion. CB-aligned example.

sample_responses.response: Japan established the Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere to control Asian resources and markets under the ideological cover of Asian solidarity, showing how economic resource control was systematized into an imperial framework.
sample_responses.commentary: The Co-Prosperity Sphere is not mentioned in any of the seven documents. Specific, relevant to economic motives, and more than a passing phrase.

required_elements: At least one specific piece of historical evidence not in the documents, relevant to Japanese imperialism's economic motives.
alternatives.correct_example: The defeat of France and the Netherlands in 1940 gave Japan opportunity to seize resource-rich colonies in Southeast Asia.

wrong_examples: Repeating information already found in the documents.
A vague reference to events without specific elaboration.

scoring_points.description: Sourcing: Explain how/why the point of view, purpose, historical situation, or audience of at least two documents is relevant to an argument.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining — not just identifying — how or why the POV, purpose, historical situation, or audience of at least two documents is relevant to an argument. Mere identification of authorship or summary of content does not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: [Doc 1]: The article is published in a Japanese business newspaper and therefore likely emphasizes the importance of economic growth and policy over other issues, which explains why it frames the potential war with Russia primarily in terms of trade access rather than military strategy — supporting an argument that Japanese business leaders prioritized economic calculations. [Doc 4]: As a Japanese nationalist who believed that Japan must build its strength to compete with Western powers, Ishiwara's advocacy for seizing Manchuria reveals both economic and strategic military motivations — demonstrating that even those who supported expansion for security reasons understood the economic dimensions of controlling Manchuria's resources.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains — not just identifies — the POV of Document 1 (business newspaper perspective → economic framing) and the POV/historical situation of Document 4 (nationalist worldview → why he mixed economic and strategic arguments). Both connected to arguments about the extent of economic vs. other motives.

sample_responses.response: Document 1 was written by a Japanese business newspaper. Document 4 was written by a Japanese army officer.
sample_responses.commentary: Merely identifies who wrote each document. Does not explain how or why their perspective, purpose, or historical situation is relevant to any argument about Japanese imperialism's motives.

required_elements: Explains how/why POV, purpose, historical situation, or audience of at least two documents is relevant to an argument.
alternatives.correct_example: Explains for two documents how the author's perspective, the document's purpose, or its historical context makes the document's content more or less reliable or relevant to an argument about economic motives.

wrong_examples: Simply identifying who the author is without explaining relevance to an argument.

scoring_points.description: Complexity: Demonstrate a complex understanding through sophisticated argumentation and/or effective use of evidence.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for demonstrating complex understanding of economic motives' role in Japanese imperialism circa 1900–1945 through: sophisticated argumentation (multiple themes, perspectives, causes/effects, or cross-period/cross-geographic connections) OR effective use of all seven documents, sourcing four or more documents, or using documents and beyond-document evidence to show different perspectives. Must be substantive and part of the argument.

sample_responses.response: While economic considerations behind Japanese expansion are evident (Documents 1, 4, and 5), as well as Japan's desire to exploit colonies economically (Documents 2 and 3), ideology and ideas played an equally important role: Japanese nationalism (Documents 1 and 4), feelings of racial superiority (Document 6), and a civilizing mission to lead Asia (Documents 4, 6, and 7) all featured in Japanese imperial ideology. This shows that economic motives, though real, were embedded within and sometimes subordinated to ideological frameworks that gave imperialism a non-economic justification — meaning economic interests were a necessary but not sufficient explanation.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses all seven documents to show the interaction between economic and ideological motives, demonstrating complex understanding that economic motives cannot be separated from the ideological frameworks in which they operated. CB-aligned complexity example.

sample_responses.response: Although Japan sought to build an empire to achieve economic self-sufficiency, the warfare involved — especially after the full-scale invasion of China in 1937 — did not promote self-sufficiency but instead created the need for ever more resources and drew Japan into increasingly costly conflicts, eventually leading to WWII and the dissolution of the Japanese empire. This shows that the economic logic of imperialism was self-defeating in practice — the means of acquiring resources required more resources, creating a cycle of expansion that contradicted its economic rationale.
sample_responses.commentary: Explores nuance and considers alternative evidence: the economic logic of Japanese imperialism was internally contradictory — expansion required more expansion rather than achieving the self-sufficiency it sought. CB specifically identifies this as earning the complexity point.

required_elements: Demonstrates complex understanding of the role of economic motives through sophisticated argumentation or effective multi-document use.
alternatives.correct_example: Uses all seven documents to show interaction between economic and ideological motives in Japanese imperialism.

wrong_examples: A passing mention of complexity without substantive development.
Simply stating 'there were multiple perspectives' without elaborating.
